critic_raw:
Review of the churn-risk model evaluation:

The telecom operator's churn model evaluation uses ROC-AUC as the sole headline metric to justify a workflow change. This is problematic because the retention team can only act on 2% of the user base per week. AUC measures model quality across the full score distribution — including the 98% of users who will never be called. A model can improve AUC substantially by better separating non-churning users in the lower deciles, with no improvement in the top 2% that actually drives retention calls.

Three evaluation gaps:
1. No capacity-constrained metric (precision@2%, recall@2%, lift@2%) — the metrics that directly assess whether the better-AUC model produces a better call list.
2. ROC-AUC is not the decision metric — the decision is threshold-constrained, and threshold-independence is not a virtue when the deployment operates at a single threshold.
3. No intervention outcome — incremental retention among called users is not reported. Ranking better is a means to an end; the end is retaining users.

The data science team's argument that AUC is 'threshold-independent and therefore more robust' is technically accurate but strategically irrelevant when the business has a fixed 2% capacity constraint.

Verdict: empirical_test_agreed — compute precision@2% and lift@2% from the test month data to resolve whether the AUC improvement translates to operational value.
adjudication_raw: Single-agent baseline identified all three must-find issues. No must-not claims raised. Verdict: empirical_test_agreed.

empirical_test.measure: precision@2% and lift@2% on held-out test month for both models
empirical_test.success_criterion: new model shows materially higher precision and lift at the 2% cutoff than the incumbent logistic model
empirical_test.failure_criterion: top-2% metrics are not improved despite the 7-point AUC gain, showing the improvement is not concentrated at the operational threshold
